title: Course Overview
text:
Exercises
The aim of this series of practical sessions is to develop the skills to independently perform biological and mathematical analyses using the programming language R. There will be eight two hour practicals, four in Michaelmas term and four in Lent term.
Exercises in these materials are labelled as follows:

title: Installing R and RStudio
text:
Installing R and RStudio
Prior to the practicals, you need to install two pieces of software on your computer - R, a programming language, and RStudio, which acts as a graphical interface for R.
Please try to do this, following the instructions below, before the first practical.
You will need: - R - at least version 4.6.1 (2026-06-24) - RStudio - at least version 2026.06.0

title: Installing R and RStudio
text:
Xcode Command Line Tools
We recommend installing the Xcode Command Line Tools, which makes several useful software utilities available.
In your terminal, run:
xcode-select --install

title: Installing R and RStudio
text:
Homebrew
On macOS the brew package manager is a popular choice for installing packages as a user.
First, make sure to install Xcode as instructed in the previous section. Then, in your terminal, run:
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
Test your brew installation by installing the wget utility, which is used to download files from the web:
brew install wget
You can now install R and RStudio using brew. In your terminal, run:
brew install --cask r
brew install --cask rstudio
Alternatively, you can manually download and install these programs using default options:

R
RStudio

title: Setting Up RStudio and GitHub
text:
Setting up Git and GitHub
↑ top
Before we start running code, we should make sure our RStudio session is set up correctly.
In this course, we will use Git and GitHub to track changes to our code and store the code online. We can use these tools directly in RStudio.
crumbs: 3  Setting up Git, GitHub and RStudio

title: Setting Up RStudio and GitHub
text:
Git






Figure 3.1: Git logo



Git is a version control system for code (and other files). It keeps track of changes you make to your files over time, allowing you to return to earlier versions if something goes wrong. It also makes it easier for multiple people to work on the same project.
Usually, Git is used to track all the changes within a folder, called a Git Repository.


GitHub






Figure 3.2: GitHub logo



GitHub is an online platform for storing Git repositories. It lets you back up your work in the cloud, share your code with others, and collaborate on projects. GitHub uses Git to track changes, but also provides additional features.


Setting up your GitHub account
Throughout this course, you’ll use Git to record the history of your code and GitHub to store a copy of your work online.
This means you’ll always have a backup of your work, you’ll be able to recover previous versions if you make a mistake, and you’ll be learning to use the same tools that are widely used in research and industry.
For the MB1A practicals, we would like you to create a free GitHub account (unless you already have an account you’re happy to use).






Important



Please set up a GitHub account now, preferably using your University email address.
You will not need Copilot for these practicals.
Feel free to turn off or reroute notifications.
When you have set up your account, please send us your CRSID and GitHub username using this form.




Forking the MB1A Students account
Once you have an account, log in and then visit the following page:
https://github.com/UoC-MB1A/MB1A_Students_2026_2027
This repository contains all of the data we will use in these practicals and will provide you with a robust, reproducible setup in which to run your code.
At the top of your GitHub browser page, you should see a button labelled Fork.

Forking a GitHub repository creates a full copy of everything in the repository inside your own account
You can change your copy without affecting the original.
If the original repository is updated, you can sync your fork to copy those changes into your own repository.

Click on the Fork button on your GitHub browser page.






Figure 3.3: The fork button



On the next page, you should see the following:






Figure 3.4: Create a new fork page



The default settings are fine for our purposes, choose Create Fork.
crumbs: 3  Setting up Git, GitHub and RStudio

title: Setting Up RStudio and GitHub
section: Set up a personal access token
text:
Set up a personal access token
↑ top
You will also need to set up a personal access token which allows your GitHub account to connect to your RStudio session.
To do so, go to the GitHub Personal Access Tokens settings page and click on Generate New Token.
Give your token the name MB1A.
In the Repository Access section, choose MB1A_Students_2026_2027






Figure 3.5: GitHub Token Choose Repo



Under Permissions choose Select repository permissions and then Contents.






Figure 3.6: GitHub Token Set Permissions



Click on Access Read-only in the Contents section and change it to Read and write






Figure 3.7: GitHub Token Read-Write



Click Generate token.
GitHub will give you a code to use as your personal access token. Do not close the page, as you’ll need this token shortly.
crumbs: 3  Setting up Git, GitHub and RStudio

title: Setting Up RStudio and GitHub
text:
Cloning a repository with RStudio
↑ top
Now we’re ready to load this repository in RStudio. You should already have installed R and RStudio on your computer, following the instructions here: Installing R and RStudio
Now we want to import our new fork of the Git repository into RStudio.
To do this, in your RStudio session, select File > New Project.






Figure 3.8: File > New Project



In the window which pops up, choose Version Control.






Figure 3.9: New Project Version Control



Select Git - Clone a project from a Git repository.






Figure 3.10: New Project Git



Copy and paste the URL of your forked repository into the Repository URL box.






Important



Make sure you are using the URL for your forked repository, not the original! Your username should be in the URL. If you’re not sure, please talk to a demonstrator.


The rest of the boxes should fill in automatically.
Click Create Project.






Figure 3.11: New Project Clone



RStudio will download a copy of your repository (known as a clone) and open it as a new project.
You now have a local copy of the practical repository on your computer, where you can edit files, save your work and upload your changes to GitHub.
To see where this folder is saved on your computer, go to the Files panel - the bottom right corner of the RStudio interface. You may need to click on the Files tab.
Click on More then Show Folder in a New Window






Figure 3.12: Find Project Folder



Inside this folder there should be a file called MB1A_Students_2026_2027.Rproj.
At the start of each practical session, you should follow the instructions below (we will remind you!). You don’t need to today, as everything should already be set up.






TipSync your Git Repository



At the beginning of each practical, make sure to sync any changes in the main copy of online repository from GitHub.

Open RStudio
Choose File > Open Project
Navigate to your MB1A_Students_2026_2027 folder and then to the directory for ?meta:practical, then load ?meta:practical.Rproj.
In a web browser, log in to GitHub and navigate to your fork of the MB1A_Students repository.
Click on Sync Fork. If it shows “This branch is not behind the upstream” there is nothing else you need to do.
Otherwise, click Update Branch. Do not click Discard commits.
Go to your RStudio session and open the Git tab.
Click Pull to update your local copy of the repository.



At the end of each session, you should follow the instructions below (again, we will remind you!).






TipPush your changes



Now you are at the end of the practical, commit your changes with Git and push them to GitHub.

Click Push in the Git panel to save the changes to GitHub.
Enter your UserName and your Personal Access Token. If you don’t have your token or it doesn’t work, visit this page while logged into your GitHub account, click on the MB1A token and choose Regenerate token.
Visit your GitHub account and check that your changes are there.



Although this seems like a lot of steps, you’ll quickly get used to the workflow. Using Git and GitHub from the start of the course will help you keep your work organised and introduce you to tools that are widely used in research and industry.

title: Part 1: Using R and RStudio
text:
The R language
↑ top






Figure 5.1: The R Logo



In this session, we will learn the basics of using the programming language R, which we will use for data analysis and visualisation throughout the MB1A course.
The R language was initially developed by two programmers, Ross Ihaka and Robert Gentleman, in 1993, for teaching introductory statistics. It has become one of the most commonly used languages in scientific computing, particularly amongst biologists.

Why learn R?
R provides many of the same functions as spreadsheet tools such as MS Excel and Google Sheets, and statistics and graphing software like SPSS and GraphPad.
However, R has lots of advantages over these tools:

It provides thousands of functions for statistics, visualisation and data analysis.
It allows you to automate repetitive tasks rather than repeating the same steps manually.
It creates reproducible workflows: every step of your analysis can be recorded, checked and repeated by yourself or others.
crumbs: Practical 1 - Basics of R and RStudio
5  Part 1: Using R and RStudio

title: Part 1: Using R and RStudio
text:
Coding in the console
↑ top
Before we go any further, let’s try writing some R code.
When you first open your RStudio project, your screen will look like this.






Figure 5.2: The welcome screen of RStudio



We will look at other parts of the screen shortly, but for now let’s focus on the Console.
In the console, R commands can be typed and executed immediately by the computer. When you type a line of code and press EnterEnter, it is transferred to the R interpreter. Results are shown straight after the command is executed. However, no record is kept and any information is lost once the session is closed.
Try typing the following, in the grey box, into the console, then press EnterEnter.

print("Hello! First contact with R successful!")

[1] "Hello! First contact with R successful!"


You should immediately see the message on the screen.
Now, try the following. Here, you are creating a new variable, x, with a value of 8. In R, the operator <- associates a specific name (here the name is x) with a specific value (we will discuss this in more detail later).

x <- 3 + 5

To see the value of x, type print (x) into the console, then press EnterEnter.

print(x)

[1] 8


Usually in these practicals, we won’t run most of our code directly in the console like this as it doesn’t save what we’ve done. Coding in the console is a bit like cooking without a recipe - the results can be great, but if you want to make the same thing again, you may not remember exactly what you did.
However, it can be useful to use the console, for example to quickly try something out or check the value of a variable.






ExerciseExercise 1








In your console, create a variable with your first name. The syntax you will need to use is as follows - replace “Mary” with your own name.


my_name <- "Mary"

Then, have R write you a message as follows:

print(paste("Hi", my_name, "Welcome to R!"))

[1] "Hi Mary Welcome to R!"












ImportantThe R prompt



If R is ready to accept commands, the R console shows a > symbol.
If the console shows a + symbol, this means it is waiting for something - you have most likely not ‘closed’ a parenthesis or quotation, i.e. you don’t have the same number of left-parentheses as right-parentheses, or the same number of opening and closing quotation marks. When this happens, and you thought you finished typing your command, click inside the console window and press EscapeEscape. This will cancel the incomplete command and return you to the > prompt.
crumbs: Practical 1 - Basics of R and RStudio

title: Part 1: Using R and RStudio
text:
The RStudio interface
↑ top
Now we will learn a bit about the rest of RStudio.
RStudio is an interactive development environment (IDE) - a piece of software designed to make working with R as easy as possible. It’s free, open-source and well-supported.






Figure 5.3: The welcome screen of RStudio



In the image above you can see that the window is divided into three main parts, each with various tabs. In clockwise order we have:

Console / Terminal / Background jobs
Environment / History / Connections / Tutorial / Git
Files / Plots / Packages / Help / Viewer / Presentation

In orange we’ve highlighted the tabs we’ll be using most.

In the Console we can directly run code, as you did above.
The Environment shows any information that is stored in R’s memory (empty in the figure above).

Click on the environment now - you should see two variables: x, with a value of 8 and my_name, with whatever you entered as your name above.






Figure 5.4: The environment tab




The Git tab allows you to interact with Git and GitHub, as we saw above.
Help is where you can go for help.







ExerciseExercise 2







In the search box on the Help tab, search for the function sum.
Using the Examples section as a guide, calculate the sum of the numbers 2, 3, 4 in the console.






AnswerAnswer







sum(2, 3, 4)

[1] 9
crumbs: Practical 1 - Basics of R and RStudio

title: Part 1: Using R and RStudio
text:
R Scripts
↑ top
The basis of programming is that we create or code instructions for the computer to follow.
Next, we tell the computer to follow the instructions by executing or running those instructions.
In your RStudio window, choose File > New File > R Script. This will add a new panel to your RStudio session, the Source panel.






Figure 5.5: The source and console panels



Here, we can save our code as a script. This is a list of commands which the R interpreter will execute in order. The script represents a complete record of what we did, and anyone (including our future selves!) can easily replicate the results on their computer. We can also use a script to repeatedly perform the same or similar tasks.







Console
Script




runs code directly
in essence, a text file


interactive
needs to be told to run


no record
records actions


difficult to trace progress
transparent workflow




Creating a script
To create a script, we put a list of commands for R to run into the Source panel.
Try entering the following into the Source panel, then press the  button. The  button runs all of the code in the Source panel, while the  button will only run the active line - the line where your cursor is currently. The answer should show in the console.

x <- 8
y <- 10
z <- x + y

print(z)

[1] 18








ExerciseExercise 3







Here is the code we wrote in the console above:

my_name <- "Mary"
print(paste("Hi", my_name, "Welcome to R!"))

[1] "Hi Mary Welcome to R!"


Write a script based on this code but to display, for example, “My name is Jeremy and I am 27 years old”, but with your own (or another) name and age. Run your script using the  button.
Save your script as my_first_script.R (using File > Save As). You can save it directly into the Practical_1_Introduction_to_R folder.






AnswerAnswer







my_name <- "Jeremy"
my_age <- 27
print(paste("My name is", my_name, "and I am", my_age, "years old"))

[1] "My name is Jeremy and I am 27 years old"

title: Part 2: Reproducible Analysis with Quarto
text:
Quarto
↑ top
Throughout this course, we will write our code in Quarto documents (with the suffix .qmd) rather than using the R Console or standalone R scripts.
Quarto allows you to create quite complex documents, for example, these teaching materials are written in Quarto.
The main reason to use Quarto is to make your research reproducible. You will likely have learned about this previously for lab-based research, but reproducibility is just as important for computer-based science.
Optional homework: There is an interesting lecture here about the importance of reproducibility in computational biology.
crumbs: Practical 1 - Basics of R and RStudio
6  Part 2: Reproducible Analysis with Quarto

title: Part 2: Reproducible Analysis with Quarto
text:
Documenting and formatting your code
↑ top

Documentation
It’s always a good idea to add explanations to your code. We can do so in Quarto, by writing a description of what we’re trying to do.
Within a code chunk, we can also use the hash tag # symbol to add comments, as shown below.

# This code calculates the sum of two numbers
1 + 9

It’s always a good idea to add lots of comments to your code. What makes sense to you in that moment, might not a week later. Similarly, when sharing code with colleagues and collaborators, it’s always good to be as clear as possible.


Formatting your code
Writing code that is well formatted - arranged neatly on the page with appropriate line breaks and spaces - makes it easier for you and others to understand, check, and reuse.
Good formatting:

Helps you to structure of your code
Makes mistakes easier to spot
Allows collaborators to quickly see what your code is doing.

In research and industry, code is often shared between people and revisited months or years later, so clear and consistent formatting is an important part of writing reproducible and maintainable analyses.
In this course, we will use the tidyverse style guide where possible. This document contains lots of references to things we haven’t covered yet. However, RStudio has an option to automatically style code, which we’ll use for now.
In the HPA Quarto document you created above, click on Addins on the menu and then Style active file - this will automatically change your code to the tidyverse style where possible.






Important



This function will only work if your code is able to run without errors and you’ve saved your Quarto document








Figure 7.3: Style Active File



For example, the Style active file feature removes the extraneous spaces from the following code.
Before:






Figure 7.4: Bad formatting



After:






Figure 7.5: Good formatting



Also, where you need to give something a name in R, it is helpful to use a name which describes what it is. For example, rather than x <- 12 it is preferable to write e.g. mean_size <- 12.






ExerciseExercise 2







Format the code in the HPA data Quarto document you created earlier using the Style active file feature. It may not change at all, or it may change a lot. Change any variable names to be informative, if they are not already.
Save the document and render it again.

title: Part 3: Functions and Variables
text:
Functions
For parts 3 and 4 of the practical, we recommend that you keep track of your progress in a new Quarto document, `Practical_1_Introduction_to_R/prac1_part3_4.qmd. You can describe the code if you wish, or just label it with the exercise number. For the data analysis exercises in Part 4: exercises 5 and 6, please create a second Quarto document Practical_1_Introduction_to_R/ukhsa_data_analysis.qmd and briefly describe your analysis.
↑ top
Functions take an input (called an argument), perform an action, and often return an output. They allow us to perform common tasks without writing the same code repeatedly.
We have used some functions already:
Functions allow us to do the same thing multiple times without rewriting code. We can also write our own functions, we’ll come to this in a later practical.
A function usually takes one or more inputs called arguments. They often (but not always) return an output.
A typical example would be the function sqrt(). The input (the argument) must be a number, and the output is the square root of that number.
sqrt(9)

[1] 3
Here, the input 9 is given as an argument to the sqrt() function. The function calculates the square root, and returns the value. This function is very simple, because it takes just one argument and has no additional options.
crumbs: Practical 1 - Basics of R and RStudio

title: Part 3: Functions and Variables
text:
Figure 7.1: Functions





The print() function outputs text to the screen.
The sum() function adds together a series of numbers.










Figure 7.2: Functions











ExerciseExercise 1







The function round() rounds a number, by default into the nearest whole number.
Write some code to round the number 3.14159 to the nearest whole number.






AnswerAnswer







round(3.14159)

[1] 3
crumbs: Practical 1 - Basics of R and RStudio

title: Part 3: Functions and Variables
text:
Arguments
↑ top
Arguments allow you to control the behaviour of a function. They can be anything, not only numbers. Exactly what each argument means differs per function and can be looked up in the documentation.
Some functions take arguments which may either be specified by the user, or, if left out, take on a default value: these are called options.
Options are typically used to alter the way the function operates.
Let’s look at the round() function again.






Figure 7.3: Functions




round(3.14159)

[1] 3


This gives us an integer, 3.






Figure 7.4: Functions



However, if we want e.g. two digits after the decimal point, we can type digits = 2 (or however many we want). For example:

round(3.14159, digits = 2)

[1] 3.14



Ordering arguments
When a function has several arguments, there are two common ways to provide them. You can either give the arguments in the correct order, or you can name each argument explicitly.
For example, let’s take the function seq().
This function generates a sequence of numbers. The arguments are, in order, from, to and by, where from is the number you want the sequence to start from, to is the number you want it to end at and by is the increment. If you search in the Help panel for seq you’ll see this order - from, to, by.
We can call the function with these three arguments:
For example:

print(seq(1, 10, 2))

[1] 1 3 5 7 9



print(seq(5, 15, 5))

[1]  5 10 15


However, we can also use their names, and then put them in any order:

print(seq(by = 2, from = 1, to = 10))

[1] 1 3 5 7 9



print(seq(by = 5, to = 15, from = 5))

[1]  5 10 15








ExerciseExercise 2







The function rep() repeats a value, x a specific number of times. The order of arguments is: rep(x, times) - so you first specify x, then the number of times you want it to be repeated.
For example:

print(rep(2, 5))

[1] 2 2 2 2 2



print(rep(x=2, times=5))

[1] 2 2 2 2 2


Write some code to repeat the number 5, 7 times, first by specifying arguments in order and then using their names.






AnswerAnswer







# ordered
print(rep(5, 7))

[1] 5 5 5 5 5 5 5

# keywords
print(rep(times = 7, x = 5))

[1] 5 5 5 5 5 5 5
crumbs: Practical 1 - Basics of R and RStudio

title: Part 3: Functions and Variables
text:
Variables
↑ top
Another fundamental concept in programming is a variable.
Basically, a variable associates a specific name with a specific value. In R, we use the operator <- to assign a value to a name, we’ve done this several times already.
For example, by running the code below, we are saying that the name my_variable refers to the value 12. When you run the code, it will seem like nothing has happened, but, interally, R now knows that my_variable is equal to 12.

my_variable <- 12

To check this has worked, we can use the function, print() to show the value of our variable on the screen.

my_variable <- 12
print(my_variable)

[1] 12


We can change the value of our variable by assigning it a new value. Although the first line of the code below is run, the value is then replaced in the second line, so when we reach the third line, the value is 10.

my_variable <- 12
my_variable <- 10
print(my_variable)

[1] 10








ExerciseExercise 3







What would be the value of whatsmynumber after running all of code below?

whatsmynumber <- 1
whatsmynumber <- 5







AnswerAnswer






5









Variable Names
You can have as many variables as you like, and use any name, with a few exceptions.
Your variable names should be one word, without spaces and without punctuation except underscores (“_“), so the following won’t work:

my-var <- 12

Error:
! object 'my' not found



my var <- 12

Error in parse(text = input): <text>:1:4: unexpected symbol
1: my var
       ^


In R, variable names can’t start with a number.

1var <- 12

Error in parse(text = input): <text>:1:2: unexpected symbol
1: 1var
     ^


Some words are used by the R language itself, so although you can use them as variable names, it can become confusing and stop other functions from working.
Some will not work at all:

if <- 12

Error in parse(text = input): <text>:1:4: unexpected assignment
1: if <-
       ^


Others will work, but cause problems. For example, if you assign a value to the word sum, then you won’t be able to calculate mean values using the R function sum.
To check if a word already exists as a function in R, you can type ? followed by the word. This will show the help screen for any existing functions.
e.g.

?sum

Compare this to a variable name which is not already in use:

?banana

No documentation for 'banana' in specified packages and libraries:
you could try '??banana'
crumbs: Practical 1 - Basics of R and RStudio

title: Part 3: Functions and Variables
text:
Types of variable
↑ top

Numeric, character and logical variables
Different types of data can be stored as variables.
There are many types of variable in R.
Some of the most important are:

Numeric
(sometimes also called double)
All variables which are numbers can be classed as numeric (there are some more specific number types but you don’t need these yet)

x <- 1
print(x)

[1] 1

x <- 1.74
print(x)

[1] 1.74

x <- 4e-3
print(x)

[1] 0.004




Character
(sometimes called a string)
A character variable consists of text - it can be one or multiple words.

x <- "hello"
print(x)

[1] "hello"

x <- "I am a variable"
print(x)

[1] "I am a variable"




Logical
(sometimes called boolean)
These can only be TRUE or FALSE.

x <- TRUE
print(x)

[1] TRUE

x <- FALSE
print(x)

[1] FALSE




Identifying types of variables
We can find out what type of variable we have using the function class.

x <- "hi there"
print(class(x))

[1] "character"

x <- 1513871
print(class(x))

[1] "numeric"

x <- FALSE
print(class(x))

[1] "logical"








ExerciseExercise 4







Create character, numerical and logical variables, print them, then check their classes.






AnswerAnswer







# for example
my_favourite_number <- 11
my_favourite_word <- "hullabaloo"
is_this_true <- FALSE

print(class(my_favourite_number))

[1] "numeric"

print(class(my_favourite_word))

[1] "character"

print(class(is_this_true))

[1] "logical"

title: Part 3: Functions and Variables
text:
Review
↑ top






NoteSummary




A function performs a specific task, usually taking one or more inputs (called arguments) and often returning an output.
Arguments provide information to a function and control how it behaves. Some arguments are optional and have default values.
A variable stores a value under a name so that it can be used again later in your code.
Choose descriptive variable names and avoid spaces, punctuation (except _), names beginning with numbers, and names that already have a meaning in R.
Common variable types include numeric (numbers), character (text) and logical (TRUE or FALSE).
The class() function tells you what type of data a variable contains.

title: Part 4: Vectors, data frames and plots
text:
Packages
↑ top
The standard version of R contains many useful packages and functions for data analysis. However, there are also thousands of additional packages containing additional functions for specific purposes.
Normally, you need to install these packages in RStudio before you use them.
Let’s install the package fortunes, to demonstrate.
install.packages("fortunes")

Installing package into '/home/katy/R/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu-library/4.6'
(as 'lib' is unspecified)
We can then load the package into R using the function library:
library(fortunes)
This package just includes one function, fortune, which outputs a random quote about R.
fortune()


For quite a while, bug-for-bug compatibility with S-PLUS v 3.x was considered
important to allow people to port their packages between systems.
   -- Peter Dalgaard
      R-help (February 2009)
You have already installed all the packages you will need for this practical by following the instructions in Installing R and RStudio.
However, we will sometimes need to load a package, for example, for this practical we will use some functions from the tidyverse package collection for data science and from the gt package for visualising data frames.
Therefore, you need to include the following code at the beginning of your Quarto document.
library(tidyverse)

title: Part 4: Vectors, data frames and plots
text:
Vectors
↑ top
All of the variables we’ve created so far just have a single element - one number, one section of text, or one TRUE/FALSE.
However, we can also create variables which contain multiple elements.
One of the most common types of multi-element variable in R is a vector.
Vectors in R are (usually) specified using the function c.

my_first_vector <- c(1, 5, 9)
print(my_first_vector)

[1] 1 5 9


You can have vectors with all numbers, all text, or all logical values, but everything needs to be the same type.

character_vec <- c("hello", "this", "is", "a", "character", "vector")
print(character_vec)

[1] "hello"     "this"      "is"        "a"         "character" "vector"   

logical_vec <- c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)
print(logical_vec)

[1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE


If you have existing variables, you can put them into a vector using their variable names.

x <- 1
y <- 2
z <- 3

xyz_vec <- c(x, y, z)







ExerciseExercise 1







Let’s look again at our dataset in which you have treated one batch of cells with radiation and kept the other as a control, then counted the number of live cells before and after.
The attributes of your two batches of cells are in this table:








cell_ID
treated
num_cells_before
num_cells_after




CX1
TRUE
5415
1432


CX2
FALSE
8123
7543







In your existing Quarto document, combine the variables you made earlier into four vectors, one for each column of the table.






AnswerAnswer







# for example
cell_id_1 <- "CX1"
cell_id_2 <- "CX2"

treated_1 <- TRUE
treated_2 <- FALSE

n_before_1 <- 5415
n_before_2 <- 8123

n_after_1 <- 1432
n_after_2 <- 7543

cell_id <- c(cell_id_1, cell_id_2)
treated <- c(treated_1, treated_2)
n_before <- c(n_before_1, n_before_2)
n_after <- c(n_after_1, n_after_2)










Accessing elements of a vector
Once our values are in a vector, we can access them using an index, which refers to the position of the value within the vector. Indices are indicated within square brackets - [].

nice_food <- c("cheese", "beans", "potatoes")

print(nice_food[1])

[1] "cheese"

print(nice_food[2])

[1] "beans"

print(nice_food[3])

[1] "potatoes"




Index Exercise






ExerciseExercise 2







Isolate the fourth value in this vector and assign it to a new variable, then print its value.

all_my_pets <- c("dog", "horse", "guinea pig", "chicken", "cat")







AnswerAnswer







# for example
all_my_pets <- c("dog", "horse", "guinea pig", "chicken", "cat")
my_only_bird <- all_my_pets[4]
print(my_only_bird)

[1] "chicken"

title: Part 4: Vectors, data frames and plots
text:
Data Frames
↑ top
Our next variable type is a data frame (sometimes called a tibble), which is one of the types of variable you’ll most commonly use. A data frame is basically a table, consisting of rows and columns. Each row is a single record, corresponding to some entity - a cell, a person, a country - and each column is a specific piece of information.
For example:








fruit
length
weight




apple
22
150


pear
30
165


banana
55
170







In this data frame, there are three columns - fruit, length and weight, and three rows, each containing information about a specific fruit.
We can build a data frame using the R function tibble (note that there is also an R function data.frame which you might see referenced elsewhere, usually modern applications use tibble as it is more user-friendly).
I would specify the data frame above as follows:

my_data_frame <- tibble(
  fruit = c("apple", "pear", "banana"),
  length = c(22, 30, 55),
  weight = c(150, 165, 170)
)


print(my_data_frame)

# A tibble: 3 × 3
  fruit  length weight
  <chr>   <dbl>  <dbl>
1 apple      22    150
2 pear       30    165
3 banana     55    170


Although we can see our data frame using the print() function, it’s not formatted particularly nicely. Instead, we can use the function gt.

my_data_frame <- tibble(
  fruit = c("apple", "pear", "banana"),
  length = c(22, 30, 55),
  weight = c(150, 165, 170)
)

gt(my_data_frame)

CSV Format
↑ top
The data we will use in many of our practicals is stored in comma-separated values (CSV) format, a plain-text file format where each line represents a row and values in columns are separated by commas.
For example, for this table:

First, I read the table and initiate a blank canvas on which to put my plot.

weather <- read_csv("data/weather_data.csv")

Rows: 9 Columns: 10
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr  (3): station_id, weather_condition, wind_direction
dbl  (6): temperature_c, humidity_pct, wind_speed_kmh, pressure_hpa, precipi...
dttm (1): observation_time

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

ggplot(weather)










Adding axes
We use another function, aes, as an argument to the ggplot function, telling R how various attributes of the data map to the “aesthetics” of the plot.
For example, here we choose which data we will represent with the x and y axes.

ggplot(weather, aes(
  x = observation_time,
  y = temperature_c
))










Adding Data
Now we can add some data to our plot. In this plotting package, ggplot, we add additional things to our plot as layers, using a + symbol.
Here, we add a line representing the data using the geom_line function.

ggplot(weather, aes(
  x = observation_time,
  y = temperature_c
)) +
  geom_line()










Adding extra features
We can add some additional features here to make the plot look nicer. For example, here I will change the colour of the line to red, add x and y axis labels and add a title.

ggplot(weather, aes(
  x = observation_time,
  y = temperature_c
)) +
  geom_line(color = "red") +
  xlab("Date") +
  ylab("Temp (C)") +
  ggtitle("Temperature by Date")

title: Part 1: Basic Data Frame Operations
text:
The dataset
↑ top
In this practical, we will first work with a published dataset to learn how to perform various analyses in R. You’ll then be asked to carry out a similar analysis independently with data from a different source.
For the worked example, we will use data from this Science paper, published in June 2026. This study tracked the migration of a songbird species, the pied flycatcher (Ficedula hypoleuca).






Figure 10.1: Pied flycatcher



One of the most surprising findings of this study was that these birds take very different routes depending on the time of year. The authors demonstrated that:

In spring, the birds migrate north from their wintering grounds in West Africa. After crossing the Sahara, they stop over near the Mediterranean before continuing to their breeding sites in the north (Fig. 1B).
In autumn, the birds take a much longer route. They migrate south from their breeding sites, but — regardless of how far east their breeding site is — they all travel via the Iberian Peninsula (Spain and Portugal) before following the west coast of Africa to their wintering grounds (Fig. 1A). Why they take this detour is currently unknown.
As a result, spring migration routes are shorter and more variable than autumn migration routes.

In our worked example, we will reproduce Figures 1A and 1B from this paper, illustrating this phenomenon.
For this practical, we recommend that you create three Quarto documents:

Practical_2_Data_Frames_and_Plots/prac2_exercises.qmd - general practice exercises
Practical_2_Data_Frames_and_Plots/worked_example.qmd - worked example
Practical_2_Data_Frames_and_Plots/visualisation_exercise.qmd - visualisation exercise

Please briefly describe your analysis and show your code in each document.






ExerciseExercise 1







Have a look at the two plots and their legends.
What are the x and y axis labels?






AnswerAnswer






x - Longitude
y - Latitude




What do the colours represent?






AnswerAnswer






The locations of the breeding populations.

Orange - Spain
Purple - Norway
Blue - Sweden
Pink - Britain
Red - Netherlands
Turquoise - western Russia
Green - Ural
Dark green - Siberia





What do the shapes represent?






AnswerAnswer






The type of site:

Circles - breeding site
Squares - non-breeding area (this is the winter location)
Filled upward triangles - autumn post-Saharan stopover location
Filled downward triangles - spring Iberian stopover location
Open downward triangles - spring westernmost location









All of the data we will use here comes from the Supplementary Data associated with this publication.
To produce this plot, we need a single data frame which contains all of the information in the plot:

Breeding site
Longitude and latitude of breeding site
Longitude and latitude of winter non-breeding site
Longitude and latitude of post-Saharan stopover
Longitude and latitude of the Iberian stopover
Longitude and latitude of the westernmost location

However, as is very common with biological datasets, the data provided with the publication is not in the ideal format for our needs. Therefore, there are various data frame operations we need to perform before we start plotting.

Starting your analysis
The first part of the data we will use for this practical contains the longitude and latitude co-ordinates for breeding sites for eight specific populations of pied flycatcher. This data comes from Supplementary Table 1 in the publication.
The file is in the data directory for Practical 2 and named breeding_site_locations.csv. Open this file in a text editor (for example NotePad, TextEdit) and examine it.

title: Part 1: Basic Data Frame Operations
text:
The pipe symbol
↑ top
We are going to make copies of these data frames with only the columns we are interested in.
To do this, we first need to learn about the pipe symbol |>. This is an operator in R which is used to link multiple steps in an analysis.
For example:

my_numbers <- c(2, 4, 6, 8)

my_numbers |> sum()

[1] 20


Here, we first make a vector of numbers, then we use this vector as the input to the function sum().
This produces the same result as:

my_numbers <- c(2, 4, 6, 8)

sum(my_numbers)

[1] 20


Pipes make it easier to perform a series of steps on the same variable:

my_numbers <- c(2, 4, 6, 8)

my_numbers |>
  sum() |>
  sqrt()

[1] 4.472136


You can keep adding additional steps to your pipe:

my_numbers <- c(2, 4, 6, 8)

my_numbers |>
  sum() |>
  sqrt() |>
  round()

[1] 4


We can assign the result to a variable using the <- operator as usual.

my_numbers <- c(2, 4, 6, 8)

my_result <- my_numbers |>
  sum() |>
  sqrt() |>
  round()

print(my_result)

[1] 4








ExerciseExercise 6






 In your Exercises Quarto document, using the pipe operator, calculate the absolute value (function abs()) of the mean (function mean() of this vector, then round it to the nearest whole number (round()). Assign the result to a variable.

input_vec <- c(5, -10, 4, -3)







AnswerAnswer







input_vec <- c(5, -10, 4, -3)

result <- input_vec |>
  mean() |>
  abs() |>
  round()

title: Part 1: Basic Data Frame Operations
text:
Filtering rows
↑ top

Comparison operators
We can also filter our data frame to just see certain rows.
To do this, one option is to use comparison operators.
For example:



Operator
Meaning




==
is equal to


!=
is not equal to


>
is greater than


>=
is greater than or equal to


<
is less than


<=
is less than or equal to



We can use these with single values as well as with data frame columns:

x <- 3

print(x == 2)

[1] FALSE


This shows FALSE because x is not equal to 2.

x <- 3

print(x == 3)

[1] TRUE


This shows TRUE because x is equal to 3.






ExerciseExercise 8

y is equal to 7
y is less than 15
y is greater than 20
y is less than or equal to 12







AnswerAnswer







y <- 12


print(y == 7)

[1] FALSE



print(y < 15)

[1] TRUE



print(y > 20)

[1] FALSE



print(y <= 12)

[1] TRUE

title: Part 2: Complex Data Frame Operations
text:
Grouping and summarising data
↑ top
At the moment, the data frame bird_breeding_exc has seven rows, one for each breeding site - as the breeding sites are small, fixed locations. However, the data frame bird_migration_exc has multiple measurements for each location for each bird population - as the numerous birds were tracked as they migrated.
For our visualisation, it’s sufficient to just take the median values for the migration locations.
To do this, we can group our data by the values in a specific column, then calculate summary statistics for each group.
We group data using the group_by() function, then summarise for each group using the summarize() function.
First, let’s group our migration frame by the Site column.
bird_migration_grouped <- bird_migration_exc |> group_by(Site)
We can’t really visualise the grouped data frame, but we can check it has worked using the glimpse function:
glimpse(bird_migration_grouped)

Rows: 94
Columns: 9
Groups: Site [7]
$ Site               <chr> "Vomb", "Vomb", "Vomb", "Vomb", "Vomb", "Vomb", "Vo…
$ Winter_Longitude   <dbl> -12.708132, -11.279293, -10.624196, -11.401071, -12…
$ Winter_Latitude    <dbl> 10.436439, 7.111185, 11.010127, 12.490454, 8.010118…
$ Stopover_Longitude <dbl> -7.1964231, -8.3202987, -4.4147850, -2.2617310, -5.…
$ Stopover_Latitude  <dbl> 40.14848, 31.58424, 34.20548, 33.37589, 34.23834, 4…
$ Iberian_Longitude  <dbl> -5.80472833, -5.72987181, -6.70771241, -3.78472031,…
$ Iberian_Latitude   <dbl> 41.51223, 42.13779, 35.72224, 35.03179, 36.17385, 4…
$ Westmost_Longitude <dbl> -16.83263, -16.07610, -18.19715, -16.57398, -15.008…
$ Westmost_Latitude  <dbl> 32.55738, 29.28441, 30.77262, 33.41686, 24.81672, 4…
We can see Groups: Site [7] at the top, which tells us our data frame is grouped into seven different sites.
group_by() does not change the data itself. It tells R how later calculations should be applied.
We can then use the function summarize() to calculate specific statistics per group and generate a new data frame containing these values.
For example, the following code creates a new data frame, lat_df, with one column Highest_Lat_Stopover, which contains the maximum (calculated with the function max()) value in the Stopover_Latitude column for each Site.
lat_df <- bird_migration_grouped |>
  summarise(Highest_Lat_Stopover = max(Stopover_Latitude, na.rm = TRUE))

title: Part 2: Complex Data Frame Operations
text:
Long and wide formats
↑ top
Now you should have a single data frame with all the data you need for your plot.
We need to perform one more transformation before we can plot our data.
Our data is currently in what is known as wide format - with a single row per site and multiple columns for each measurement for that site. So, all our measurements for each site are on a single row in the data frame.
The plots we want to create will be simpler if we convert the data to long format, where, instead, each measurement has a row and there are multiple rows per site.






Figure 11.1: Long and wide format



We can convert our data frame to long format using the pivot_longer() function.
This is a little more complicated than some of the other functions we have used.
Basically we are instructing R to:

Select the columns to reshape: take any columns ending in _Longitude or _Latitude using cols = ends_with(c("_Longitude", "_Latitude")).
Split the column names: separate the original column names wherever there is an underscore using names_sep = "_".
Create new column names: use the first part of the original column name to create a new column called Location_Type, and use the second part (Longitude or Latitude) to create new columns containing the values. This is specified with names_to = c("Location_Type", ".value").

Single row example:
Before:

Breeding_Longitude
Breeding_Latitude
Winter_Longitude
Winter_Latitude

50
20
40



After:

Location_Type
Longitude
Latitude

10

A
Winter
20
40



We won’t ask you to do this in the visualisation exercise, but it’s needed in this case.






NoteInclude this code



Include this code chunk in your worked example Quarto document to convert your data frame from wide to long format.

combined_bird_piv <- combined_bird_data |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = ends_with(c("_Longitude", "_Latitude")),
    names_sep = "_",
    names_to = c("Location_Type", ".value")
  )

text:
Subsetting your data frame
In order to create the two plots shown in Figure 1A and Figure 1B in the publication, we need to make two subsets of our data frame, one with only the spring migration data and one with only the autumn migration data.
The spring migration data has the following values in the “Location_Type” column:

Breeding
Stopover
Winter

The autumn migration data has the following values in the “Location_Type” column:

Breeding
Iberian
Westmost
Winter

We can pass a vector of values to the filter() function and use the operator %in% to filter rows on multiple values.
For example, let’s go back to our Department data frame.

gt(df_dept)

keep_depts <- c("Maths", "English")

df_eng_maths <- df_dept |>
  filter(Department %in% keep_depts)

ExerciseExercise 3







In your worked example Quarto document, generate two subsets of your combined_bird_piv data frame, one with the spring data and one with the winter data. Store these in two variables spring_data and autumn_data.






AnswerAnswer







spring_vals <- c("Breeding", "Stopover", "Winter")
autumn_vals <- c("Breeding", "Iberian", "Westmost", "Winter")

spring_data <- combined_bird_piv |>
  filter(Location_Type %in% spring_vals)

autumn_data <- combined_bird_piv |>
  filter(Location_Type %in% autumn_vals)









We now have our two input tables for the plots!
We can save copies using the function write_csv(). The first argument is the variable we want to save and the second is the path to the file we want to save it into.

Include this code chunk in your worked example Quarto document to save your data frame into the output folder for the practical.

write_csv(spring_data, "output/birds_spring.csv")
write_csv(autumn_data, "output/birds_autumn.csv")

title: Part 3: Visualisation
text:
Initiating a new plot
↑ top
We will visualise the spring migration paths of the birds using the R plotting package ggplot.
We can do this in the same Quarto document used for Part 1.
ggplot builds figures by starting with a blank plot and then adding layers. We first tell ggplot which data frame to use, then specify the variables to plot, and finally add layers such as points, lines and axes.
As we saw in Practical 1 (Plotting in R), we first need to generate a new blank plot.
We do this using the function ggplot(), with our data frame as an argument.

ggplot(spring_data)








Based on the published plot, we know that our x axis should be Longitude and our y axis should be Latitude.






ExerciseExercise 1







Look back at the plotting code from Practical 1 (Plotting in R).
In your worked example Quarto document, generate a new blank plot then add the x and y axis.






AnswerAnswer







ggplot(
  spring_data,
  aes(x = Longitude, y = Latitude)
)

title: Part 3: Visualisation
text:
Adding points
↑ top
A plot of longitude against latitude is simply a scatter plot - longitude determines the position on the x axis and latitude determines the position on the y axis.
We can add points to our plot using the geom_point() function, just like we used geom_line() to add a line in the previous practical (Plotting in R).
To demonstrate the geom_point() function, let’s briefly revisit our fruit data frame from the previous practical.

my_data_frame <- tibble(
  fruit = c("apple", "pear", "banana"),
  length = c(22, 30, 55),
  weight = c(150, 165, 170)
)

gt(my_data_frame)







fruit
length
weight




apple
22
150


pear
30
165


banana
55
170







To plot this data as a scatter plot, with length on the x axis and weight on the y axis, we would do this:

ggplot(my_data_frame, aes(x = length, y = weight)) +
  geom_point()














ExerciseExercise 2







Going back to your worked example Quarto document, add a layer to your existing plot to plot all of the longitude and latitude points as a scatter plot.






AnswerAnswer







ggplot(
  spring_data,
  aes(x = Longitude, y = Latitude)
) +
  geom_point()
















If you compare this with Fig 1B in the paper, you can see that the points are at the same positions.
Now, we can add some additional information to make the plot more informative.

Adding map features
ggplot has a convenient function annotation_borders() to add geographical country borders to a scatter plot representing a map. We’re also going to add a second function used with map data, coord_quickmap() - this keeps the shape of the plot consistent so the map isn’t skewed.
We can add this layer to our scatter plot as follows. The argument linewidth sets the width of the lines in the map borders.






Important



The majority of the steps in this practical are useful for any scatter plot, however, this specific code chunk is specific to plotting maps.








NoteInclude this code



In your worked example Quarto document, add this layer (starting at annotation_borders) to your existing scatterplot to add map borders.

ggplot(
  spring_data,
  aes(x = Longitude, y = Latitude)
) +
  geom_point() +
  annotation_borders(linewidth = 0.2) +
  coord_quickmap()

title: Part 3: Visualisation
text:
Setting axis limits
↑ top
It would be clearer to zoom in just on the region containing the points, we can do this by setting the axis limits.
It would be helpful to know the minimum and maximum longitude and latitude of the points, we can do this with the min() and max() functions and the function pull(), which pulls a single column from a data frame.
e.g. to find the minimum longitude in our data frame

min_long <- spring_data |>
  pull(Longitude) |>
  min()

print(min_long)

[1] -13.54433








ExerciseExercise 3







In your worked example Quarto document, before your plotting code, calculate the minimum longitude, maximum longitude and the minimum and maximum latitude, and store these as variables min_lon, max_lon, min_lat and max_lat.






AnswerAnswer







min_lon <- spring_data |>
  pull(Longitude) |>
  min()
max_lon <- spring_data |>
  pull(Longitude) |>
  max()
min_lat <- spring_data |>
  pull(Latitude) |>
  min()
max_lat <- spring_data |>
  pull(Latitude) |>
  max()


print(min_lon)

[1] -13.54433



print(max_lon)

[1] 84.6



print(min_lat)

[1] 4.093563



print(max_lat)

[1] 60










We can use these numbers directly as our axis limits - but let’s subtract 2 from each minimum and add 2 to each maximum so our points are not right on the edge of the plot.
We can set the x axis limits as an argument to the coord_quickmap() function as follows:






Important



For non-map data, we would most commonly use coord_cartesian() instead of coord_quickmap() here, which works in the same way.



ggplot(
  spring_data,
  aes(x = Longitude, y = Latitude)
) +
  geom_point() +
  annotation_borders(linewidth = 0.2) +
  coord_quickmap(xlim = c(min_lon - 2, max_lon + 2))














ExerciseExercise 4







Add x and y axis limits to the plot in your worked example Quarto document, by adding your min_lat and max_lat and min_lon and max_lon variables as arguments to coord_quickmap().






AnswerAnswer







ggplot(
  spring_data,
  aes(x = Longitude, y = Latitude)
) +
  geom_point() +
  annotation_borders(linewidth = 0.2) +
  coord_quickmap(
    xlim = c(min_lon - 2, max_lon + 2),
    ylim = c(min_lat - 2, max_lat + 2)
  )

We want to set the colour by the Site column and the shape by the Location_Type column.
To set colour by Site, we add color = Site as an argument to the aes() function.

ggplot(
  spring_data,
  aes(x = Longitude, y = Latitude, color = Site)
) +
  geom_point() +
  annotation_borders(linewidth = 0.2) +
  coord_quickmap(
    xlim = c(min_lon - 2, max_lon + 2),
    ylim = c(min_lat - 2, max_lat + 2)
  )








Setting shape by Location_Type is very similar, we add shape = Location_Type as an argument to the aes() function.






ExerciseExercise 5







In the plotting code in your worked example Quarto document, add the shape = Location_Type and color = Site arguments to the aes() function, to set both the shape and the colour of the points.

  spring_data,
  aes(x = Longitude, y = Latitude, color = Site, shape = Location_Type)
) +
  geom_point() +
  annotation_borders(linewidth = 0.2) +
  coord_quickmap(
    xlim = c(min_lon - 2, max_lon + 2),
    ylim = c(min_lat - 2, max_lat + 2)
  )

title: Part 3: Visualisation
text:
Adding paths
↑ top
Finally, we want to add paths to join the points from the same location.
To do this, we use the geom_path() function, grouping the points by breeding site, by adding the argument aes(group = Site):

ggplot(
  spring_data,
  aes(x = Longitude, y = Latitude, color = Site, shape = Location_Type)
) +
  geom_point() +
  annotation_borders(linewidth = 0.2) +
  coord_quickmap(
    xlim = c(min_lon - 2, max_lon + 2),
    ylim = c(min_lat - 2, max_lat + 2)
  ) +
  geom_path(aes(group = Site))














ExerciseExercise 6







In the plotting code in your worked example Quarto document, add the geom_path() function with the argument aes(group = Site) to your plotting code.






AnswerAnswer







ggplot(
  spring_data,
  aes(x = Longitude, y = Latitude, color = Site, shape = Location_Type)
) +
  geom_point() +
  annotation_borders(linewidth = 0.2) +
  coord_quickmap(
    xlim = c(min_lon - 2, max_lon + 2),
    ylim = c(min_lat - 2, max_lat + 2)
  ) +
  geom_path(aes(group = Site))
















There are various tweaks available if you want to change the appearance of your plot.
For example:

To change the size of the points, add e.g. size = 3 as an argument to the geom_point() function.
To change the width of the line, add e.g. linewidth = 0.5 as an argument to the geom_line() function.
To change the colour theme of all the data points, add an additional layer with scale_color_brewer(palette = “PALETTE”) , changing PALETTE to anything on the chart on this page.
You can change the overall theme of your plot using one of the theme options on this page
You can add a title by adding a layer ggtitle(), with the title as the only argument.

It’s worthwhile to search online for other aesthetic changes you can make to your plot, many options are available.
Add any of these that you like to the plotting code in your worked example Quarto document.
E.g.

ggplot(
  spring_data,
  aes(x = Longitude, y = Latitude, color = Site, shape = Location_Type)
) +
  geom_point(size = 4) +
  annotation_borders(linewidth = 0.2) +
  coord_quickmap(
    xlim = c(min_lon - 2, max_lon + 2),
    ylim = c(min_lat - 2, max_lat + 2)
  ) +
  geom_path(aes(group = Site), size = 1) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  theme_bw() +
  ggtitle("Spring Migration Routes")

Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.









Now we have our plot! Compare it to Figure 1B in the publication.






ExerciseExercise 7







In your worked example Quarto document, repeat all of the steps above with your autumn_data data frame, to generate your own version of Figure 1A.

title: Part 4: Visualisation Exercise
text:
Load packages
In this exercise, you will apply the skills from Parts 1 to 3 to analyse a new dataset independently. You will download animal tracking data from the Movebank database, process it into a suitable format, and create a map showing the median location of each tracked individual on each day.
Movebank is a free, online database of animal tracking data hosted by the Max Planck Institute of Animal Behavior. It is a database of data from lots of different animal tracking studies, all stored in a standardised format.
For this part of the practical, you will choose an animal tracking dataset and plot the tracked locations of different individuals within that dataset on a map.
↑ top
Please start a new Quarto document for this exercise, visualisation_exercise.qmd.
We need to load the tidyverse and gt packages again, together with two additional packages:

janitor, which helps clean column names.
lubridate, which provides functions for working with dates.

library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(janitor)
library(lubridate)

↑ top

To find your dataset:

Agree with the terms and conditions
Download the data in CSV format

Read data

Read the data frame into a variable.
This data has a couple of features you’ve not come across yet, so we’ll provide some code to help you:

The column names are not in a format that R can easily use, as they contain hyphens (-). We can use an additional function, clean_names() to rename the columns. clean_names() converts column names into a consistent format by replacing spaces and punctuation with underscores and converting everything to lower case.
One of the columns contains date information which needs to be reformated - we do this using the mutate() function and the as_date() function. We’ll learn more about the mutate() function in a later practical.

title: Part 1: Introduction to Bioconductor and Sequence Analysis in R
text:
Endogenous retroviruses
↑ top
Retroviruses are RNA viruses that replicate by converting their RNA genome into DNA. As part of their life cycle, retroviruses integrate this DNA into the genome of their vertebrate host, inserting a copy of their retroviral genome into the host genome.






Figure 15.1: Retrovirus integration



Usually, this happens in a somatic cell, and the virus dies with the host. However, occasionally by chance it will happen in a germline cell, in which case the retrovirus can be inherited as part of the host genome. These inherited retroviruses are known as endogenous retroviruses (ERVs).
Although integration into a germline cell is rare for any individual virus infection, over millions of years of evolution these events accumulate and become relatively common, for example, 5% of the human genome is derived from ERVs.
Endogenous retroviruses are interesting partly because, after integration, they evolve at the slow evolutionary rate of the host instead of the very fast rate we see for viruses. This means that integrated historic viruses can still resemble the original circulating virus for millions, or even hundreds of millions of years. Endogenous retroviruses are one of our best sources of information about the evolutionary history of retroviruses and are sometimes referred to as “molecular fossils” of historical viruses.
Endogenous retroviruses in humans are fairly well known, but we know almost nothing about the retroviruses in the genomes of other species. For example, our recent analyses have revealed that the genome of the North American beaver, Castor canadensis, contains a very large number of endogenous retroviruses, but nobody has looked at these in detail.






Figure 15.2: Castor canadensis
crumbs: 15  Part 1: Introduction to Bioconductor and Sequence Analysis in R

title: Part 1: Introduction to Bioconductor and Sequence Analysis in R
text:
The Practical
↑ top
The aim of this practical is to generate new biological knowledge by analysing endogenous retrovirus sequences from the North American beaver, using bioinformatics techniques. We will upload the annotations generated during this practical to a new publicly available database of mammalian endogenous retroviruses, for the research community to use in the future.

Input
Our start point will be regions of the beaver genome which have previously been identified as likely containing ERVs. Each student will characterise one ERV region, and together the class will build a larger collection of well-characterised ERVs.


Steps
In order to characterise the ERVs we will:

Isolate the sequences from the ERV-like regions
Identify open reading frames (ORFs) in the sequences.
Find out if these ORFs resemble retroviral genes, and if so, which genes.
Find the closest related retroviruses using BLAST and build a multiple sequence alignment and phylogenetic tree
Estimate the age of the ERV using a pairwise sequence alignment.



Output
At the end of the practical you can upload your results to a public database to be used by the research community.

text:
Bioconductor
↑ top
Bioconductor is a repository for R packages, datasets and workflows which are specific for analysing biological data.






Figure 15.3: Bioconductor logo



We are going to use several Bioconductor packages for biological sequence analysis, Biostrings, ORFik, msa, ggtree.
Import these libraries into your Quarto document. We will also need the gt and tidyverse libraries.

library(Biostrings)
library(ORFik)
library(msa)
library(ggtree)
library(gt)
library(tidyverse)

title: Part 1: Introduction to Bioconductor and Sequence Analysis in R
section: Isolate sequences from the ERV-like regions
text:
Isolate sequences from the ERV-like regions
↑ top
You have been provided with sequence data from the beaver genome in FASTA format as data/C_canadensis_erv_regions.fasta. This file contains the DNA sequence of 365 regions of the beaver genome which have previously been identified as potentially containing ERVs.
FASTA is a simple text-based format commonly used for storing biological sequences, such as DNA, RNA or protein sequences.
Each sequence in a FASTA file consists of two parts:

A sequence identifier (or name), which begins with a > symbol.
On the following line or lines, the biological sequence itself.

A FASTA file can one or many contain many sequences. Each sequence starts with a new identifier line followed by a line break, then its sequence, then another line break.
e.g. 
>Sequence1
AGCTATGCTAGCTAGCTAGCTA
>Sequence2
TTCAGCTGATTCTGATCTACTA
Open the FASTA file provided in the data directory as data/C_canadensis_erv_regions.fasta in a text editor on your computer.






ExerciseExercise 1







What is the name of the second sequence in the file?






AnswerAnswer






Ccan_ERV_2








First, we want to read the FASTA file. The Biostrings function readDNAStringSet can do this.

beaver_fasta <- readDNAStringSet("data/C_canadensis_ervs.fasta")

This reads our FASTA file into a variable type called a DNAStringSet, which is specifically designed for working with DNA sequences.

print(beaver_fasta)

  [5] 11319 TTCAGGCTGACCTCGAACTGAG...TTTCAACTAATTCACTATTTTT Ccan_ERV_205
  ...   ... ...
[361] 21761 AGCATGGGTGCTTATACCAGGA...TGAAAAAAAAAAACTCACTTAG Ccan_ERV_239
[362] 11733 AGACCATTTCTTTTGATGAACA...ACAAAACATTCCTACATTTCAC Ccan_ERV_336

[365] 11151 TATGTGGACATTAGATCAAGGG...AAGGACAGGTTAGTGGAGTGGC Ccan_ERV_337








ExerciseExercise 2







You can access the lengths of the sequences using the function width(), with your DNAStringSet variable as the argument.
Using R functions, find out:

How long is the longest sequence?
How long is the shortest sequence?
What is the mean sequence length?







AnswerAnswer






How long is the longest sequence?

print(max(width(beaver_fasta)))

[1] 41102


How long is the shortest sequence?

print(min(width(beaver_fasta)))

[1] 7590


What is the mean sequence length?

print(mean(width(beaver_fasta)))

[1] 12226.73










In the spreadsheet here there is a list of the beaver ERV regions - the same ones that are in your FASTA file. As a group, we want to try to analyse as many of these as possible. Each student will characterise one ERV region, and together the class will build a larger collection of annotated ERVs.






Important



Please choose any available region and put your CRSID next to it on the spreadsheet, so others know that it is taken. Each sequence has a unique ID, please note the ID for your sequence. Wherever you are asked later for your ERV_ID, this is the number you need.


Access your specific sequence using this unique ID as an index - beaver_fasta["UNIQUE_ID"] where UNIQUE_ID is the ID for your sequence.
e.g. 

current_erv <- beaver_fasta["Ccan_ERV_1"]

print(current_erv)

title: Part 2: Finding Open Reading Frames
text:
Retrovirus genes
↑ top
Now we have our sequence, we want to check if it really does contain an ERV.
All retroviruses have three genes in common (some also have additional genes):
When the retrovirus first enters the host genome, it will be identical to the viral genome. However, over time, the retrovirus will pick up mutations and become less and less recognisable.
crumbs: 16  Part 2: Finding Open Reading Frames

title: Part 2: Finding Open Reading Frames
text:
gag - group-specific antigen - encodes structural proteins (1500-1800 nt).
pol - polymerase - encodes enzymes required for viral replication (2500-3500 nt).
env - envelope - encodes surface proteins which allow the virus to attach to host cells (2500-3000 nt).
crumbs: 16  Part 2: Finding Open Reading Frames

title: Part 2: Finding Open Reading Frames
text:
Open Reading Frames
↑ top
When a retrovirus first integrates into the host genome, its copy in the host genome will contain intact, full length open reading frames (ORFs) for at least gag, pol and env.
An ORF is a stretch of DNA that could potentially encode a protein. It typically begins with a start codon and continues until the next stop codon.
When we translate a sequence, it can be translated in 3 reading frames on each strand.
For example, lets say we have the sequence below:

my_seq <- DNAStringSet("ATATGAAACCCGGGTTTTAAGC")







Note



In reality, DNA is transcribed into RNA before being translated, however for convenience in R we can directly translate DNA sequences.


Our sequence can be divided into codons as follows:
frame 1:
ATA TGA AAC CCG GGT TTT AAG C
 I   *   N   P   G   F   K

frame 2:
AT ATG AAA CCC GGG TTT TAA GC
    M   K   P   G   F   *
frame 3:
A TAT GAA ACC CGG GTT TTA AGC
   Y   E   T   R   V   L   S
Here, the only open reading frame is in frame 2, where there is a start codon, ATG, encoding methionine (M), followed by four more amino acids, then a stop codon (*), TAA.
We can detect ORFs in R using the findORFs() function from the package ORFik.

# the [[1]] here is because this function can work on multiple
# sequences at once - we only have one sequence so we only want
# the first result.
my_orfs <- findORFs(my_seq)[[1]]

print(my_orfs)

IRanges object with 1 range and 0 metadata columns:
          start       end     width
      <integer> <integer> <integer>
  [1]         3        20        18


The result is an IRanges object which shows us there is an ORF running from the start codon at position 3 to the stop codon at position 20, with a width of 18 nucleotides.

ORFs in the ERV sequence






ExerciseExercise 1







Find ORFs in your ERV sequence (stored in the variable current_erv) using the findORFs function. As in the example, you will need to add [[1]] at the end, as you only have a single sequence.
The longest ORFs in the sequence are likely to represent the retroviral genes, so we can add the argument minimumLength = 200 to filter out ORFs less than 200 amino acids in length.
Store the result in a variable named orfs.






AnswerAnswer







orfs <- findORFs(current_erv, minimumLength = 200)[[1]]









Have a look at your orfs variable, it is an IRanges object showing the start and end positions of the ORFs within the longer sequence, along with their width (the length of the ORF in nucleotides).

print(orfs)

IRanges object with 5 ranges and 0 metadata columns:
          start       end     width
      <integer> <integer> <integer>
  [1]      2287      2964       678
  [2]      3638      4327       690
  [3]      7688      8410       723
  [4]      1401      2321       921
  [5]      5307      6008       702




Adding sequence names
Currently, our ORFs don’t have names, so it may be hard to identify which is which later.
The paste0 function in R concatenates two or more arguments into a single character variable.
e.g.

print(paste0("abc", 1))

[1] "abc1"


If one of the arguments to paste0 is a vector, R will combine each element of the vector with the other arguments.






Note



Remember that the function seq(start, end, increment) generates a vector of numbers from start to end with a step size of increment, e.g. seq(1, 10, 2) = 1, 3, 5, 7, 9.


e.g. 

print(paste0("abc", seq(1, 10)))

 [1] "abc1"  "abc2"  "abc3"  "abc4"  "abc5"  "abc6"  "abc7"  "abc8"  "abc9" 
[10] "abc10"








ExerciseExercise 2







Generate a vector to use as unique names for your ORFs using the paste0 function. They should be named ‘Ccan_ERV_j_ORF_i’ where j is the ID of your specific ERV region and i is a unique number for each ORF. You can find out how many ORFs you have using the function length(orfs) and generate the sequence of numbers using seq(). Store the names as a variable orf_names.






AnswerAnswer







# Replace ERV_1 with the ID of your specific ERV region
orf_names <- paste0("Ccan_ERV_1_ORF_", seq(1, length(orfs)))









You can assign these names to the ORFs in your IRanges variable as follows:

names(orfs) <- orf_names

Have another look at the resulting variable - the ORFs should now have unique names.

print(orfs)

IRanges object with 5 ranges and 0 metadata columns:
                       start       end     width
                   <integer> <integer> <integer>
  Ccan_ERV_1_ORF_1      2287      2964       678
  Ccan_ERV_1_ORF_2      3638      4327       690
  Ccan_ERV_1_ORF_3      7688      8410       723
  Ccan_ERV_1_ORF_4      1401      2321       921
  Ccan_ERV_1_ORF_5      5307      6008       702


Now we can retrieve the sequences for our ORFs from the longer ERV sequence we got from our FASTA file.
To do so, we can use the function extractAt().

# Again this function can work on multiple sequences at once
# We only have one sequence so we only want the first result, hence the [[1]].
orf_seqs <- extractAt(current_erv, orfs)[[1]]

print(orf_seqs)

DNAStringSet object of length 5:
    width seq                                               names               
[1]   678 CTGACTCACCCCTCCAGTTCGGC...CCACAACTCTCCAACGGCCATAG Ccan_ERV_1_ORF_1
[2]   690 ATGAGCAGTAGAGCAGGCCAGAG...TGCGGCCTTGTCGGTCTGCCTAG Ccan_ERV_1_ORF_2
[3]   723 CTGCTCAAATTTGCCTTTGTAAA...TTATTAACGCCGGTTCTACCTAG Ccan_ERV_1_ORF_3
[4]   921 CTGCGTGTCCGTGTGGGAGCTCG...TCCAGTTCGGCTCACGGGGTTAA Ccan_ERV_1_ORF_4
[5]   702 ATGCTAGTGAGAAAAGCCGATAA...AAGACGCCGAAAGCTGTCGGTAA Ccan_ERV_1_ORF_5

Translation
↑ top
Now we have our open reading frame sequences. Given that they are open reading frames, they should translate into amino acid sequences with no issues - each should start with a start codon and end with a stop codon, and there should be no internal stop codons.






ExerciseExercise 3







Translate your open reading frames into amino acid sequences. The Bioconductor function translate() will do this, with just one argument, your ORF nucleotide sequences.
Store the result in a variable named orf_aa_seqs. Look at your amino acid sequences using the print() function.






AnswerAnswer







orf_aa_seqs <- translate(orf_seqs)

print(orf_aa_seqs)

title: Part 3: Identifying Sequences with BLAST
text:
Finding related sequences using BLAST
↑ top
Now we have our amino acid sequences, we want to check if they are derived from retroviruses. We can do this using the online BLAST server, with the BLASTp algorithm, which compares protein sequences with protein sequences.
We will compare our ORF sequences with a database of known retrovirus proteins to find out:
A FASTA file of the amino acid sequences of known retroviral genes has been provided for you in data/known_ERV_proteins.fasta.
On the BLASTp homepage, first select the checkbox “Align 2 or more sequences”. This is because both our query sequences and our database sequences are stored in local FASTA files. The alternative is to search against one of the large online databases provided by NCBI.
Your query sequences are the sequences you want to identify - your putative ERV ORFs. Click the “Browse…” button in the query sequence section and choose the FASTA file you just created - output/orf_aa_seqs.fasta.
The subject sequences are the database you would like to compare to - the known retrovirus gene sequences. Click the “Browse…” button in the subject sequence section and choose the FASTA file of known retrovirus sequences - data/known_ERV_proteins.fasta.
Otherwise, the default options are OK. Click the “BLAST” button.
crumbs: 17  Part 3: Identifying Sequences with BLAST

title: Part 3: Identifying Sequences with BLAST
text:
Which retroviral genes we can identify.
What type of retrovirus they are derived from.
What are the most closely related known retroviruses?









Figure 17.1: The BLASTp homepage











Figure 17.2: BLASTp setup
crumbs: 17  Part 3: Identifying Sequences with BLAST

title: Part 3: Identifying Sequences with BLAST
text:
BLAST Results
↑ top
Once BLAST has finished running, you should see something like this:






Figure 17.3: BLASTp results



The dropdown box next to “Results for” allows you to choose which of your ORFs you would like to see the results for.






Note



It’s possible that some of your ORFs will have no results, if so that’s fine - that just means that specific ORF is unlikely to be derived from a retrovirus.


Further down on the page, you should see a table showing all of the database sequences (or the top 100, if there are more than 100) which were significantly more similar to your query sequence than would be expected by chance.






Figure 17.4: BLASTp table



The columns in this table are as follows:







Column
Meaning




Cluster Representative Sequence
The ID and name of this subject sequence.


Max Score
The highest local alignment score for a single high-scoring segment between your query and this subject sequence. Higher is better.


Total Score
The combined score of all aligned segments between your query and this subject sequence. Higher is generally better.


Query Cover
The percentage of your query sequence that is included in the alignment. Higher is better.


E value
The expected number of matches of this quality that could occur by chance in a database of this size. Lower is better; values close to zero indicate more significant matches.


Per. Ident
The percentage of aligned positions that are identical between your query and the matching sequence. Higher is better.


Acc. Len
The total length of this subject sequence in the database.


Accession
The unique identifier for this subject sequence in the database, which can be used to retrieve more information about it.



When evaluating BLAST results, you should consider several columns together. In general, a good match will have a high query cover, high percentage identity, and a low E value.
The subject retrovirus sequence names also provide some information. They are formatted as identifier|name|genus|gene, where:

identifier is a unique identifer for the sequence
name is the name of the retrovirus
genus is gamma, beta, alpha, epsilon, delta, lenti or spuma - these are the different genera of retroviruses - the taxonomic groups to which they belong.
gene is gag, pol or env - the retroviral gene which matches this ORF

For example, if I had the results below:






Figure 17.5: BLASTp table



Then my results for this ORF are:

Most similar sequence name: Bat endogenous retrovirus
Most similar sequence accesion: JQ303225
Most similar retroviral genus: gamma
Most similar retroviral gene: pol







ExerciseExercise 1







Record the most similar sequence name for each ORF in an R data frame with two columns - name and most_similar_sequence_id (as we did in Data Frames). Name the data frame variable blast_res.






AnswerAnswer







blast_res <- tibble(
  name = names(orfs),
  most_similar_sequence_id = c(
    "JQ303225|Bat_Endogenous_Retrovirus|gamma|gag",
    "JQ303225|Bat_Endogenous_Retrovirus|gamma|pol",
    "AY099324|Porcine_ERV_B|gamma|env",
    "JQ303225|Bat_Endogenous_Retrovirus|gamma|gag",
    "JQ303225|Bat_Endogenous_Retrovirus|gamma|gag"
  )
)















Important



At this point, please save the BLAST results from the web server onto your computer in FASTA format.
To do this, for each of your query sequences, select Download at the top of the hit table, then select FASTA (aligned clusters).
 This will save a file on your computer named seqdump.txt Move this file to your output folder for this practical and rename it as Ccan_ERV_j_ORF_i_refs.fasta, replacing j with your ERV ID and i with your ORF ID.
You’ll need to repeat this process for each of your ORFs.
